name: Operation Invoker
description: Interface for invoking operations described by an OpenBindings interface document. The operation invoker is the by-reference invocation layer: the caller hands it an interface and a key — an operation key (the invoker selects a binding) or a specific binding key — and the invoker dereferences that key against the document, selects a binding, validates against the operation's schemas, applies its transforms, and drives the underlying binding-invoker. It is the peer of binding-invoker, which invokes by value (a self-contained source + ref, no document). Both share one frame protocol and bottom out in the same wire engine; they differ only in how the call is addressed. Context is not owned here: the operation layer forwards to the selected binding only the context that binding's challenge requires, never broader caller context, where target-keyed resolution and the CONTEXT_REQUIRED challenge live. Binding selection is this interface's own configuration point: a normative default policy (non-deprecated before deprecated; higher declared preference first, undeclared below all declared; lexicographic binding key as the final tiebreak, over the bindings whose governing binding specification the invoker can act on) that consumer configuration may override and an explicit binding key bypasses. Conformance to this interface is claimed and versioned independently of core OpenBindings conformance and of every binding specification.

BindingContext.description: Opaque runtime context for binding invocation, typically supplied by the runtime from its store. Well-known credential fields: 'bearerToken' (string), 'apiKey' (string), 'apiKeys' (object mapping a requirement's 'name' to its key — the scheme-scoped form for the alternative that carries several API keys at once; a scheme looks up its named entry first and falls back to the single 'apiKey' convenience), 'basic' (object with 'username' and 'password'), 'accessToken' / 'refreshToken' / 'expiresAt' for OAuth. Well-known transport fields: 'headers' / 'cookies' / 'environment' (objects with string values) and 'metadata' (object with arbitrary values). Well-known configuration field: 'configuration' (object) — per-invocation configuration-point values, keyed by point name: this interface's 'selection' point, and the named points each governing binding specification defines (decode, classify, route, solicit, server, address, target, transport, per family), with values as each point's definition specifies; entries are consulted at the per-invocation tier of the point's consultation order. Implementations and callers MAY add additional fields for session state, consent flags, or any other invoker-managed runtime data; drivers SHOULD ignore fields they don't recognize.

ContextRequirement.description: One prerequisite that must be satisfied before a binding can be invoked, resolved into context. `type` names the requirement family; the remaining fields are type-specific. The well-known `auth.*` family resolves into BindingContext credential fields: `auth.bearer` produces 'bearerToken', `auth.apiKey` produces 'apiKey' (or a scheme-keyed entry in 'apiKeys' when the requirement carries a `name`), `auth.basic` produces 'basic', and `auth.oauth2` produces 'accessToken' (carrying flow fields such as 'grantType', 'authorizeUrl', 'tokenUrl', and 'scopes'). Other families (e.g. 'approval.user', 'config.value', 'account.link') resolve into their own context fields. A runtime dispatches each requirement to a resolver registered for its `type`.

type.description: Requirement type identifier, e.g. 'auth.bearer', 'auth.oauth2', 'auth.basic', 'auth.apiKey', 'approval.user', 'config.value'. Resolvers register against these; an unrecognized type is unsatisfiable by a runtime that has no resolver for it. An invoker whose source artifact declares a scheme it cannot itself apply still surfaces the requirement with a type derived from the artifact's scheme (e.g. 'auth.http.digest'), so the alternative stays discoverable rather than silently dropped — the surfaced-never-skipped rule each binding specification pins for its own credential carriage.

name.description: The scheme name as the source artifact declares it (e.g. an OpenAPI securitySchemes key). Distinguishes two requirements of the same type within one alternative — two ANDed API keys are otherwise indistinguishable — and keys scheme-scoped credential lookup (see BindingContext's 'apiKeys').

description.description: Human-readable description shown to a user or operator during resolution (e.g. an approval prompt message).

durable.description: Whether resolved context for this requirement MAY be persisted under a storage key the runtime derives from the challenge `target` and reused for later invocations (true; e.g. credentials), or MUST be satisfied freshly for every invocation and MUST NOT be persisted (false; e.g. a one-shot user approval). Defaults to true. A requirement family typically fixes this value.

ContextRequiredDetails.description: The `details` payload of an InvocationError whose `code` is CONTEXT_REQUIRED, and the return shape of prepareBinding. Enumerates the runtime context the invoker needs before it can proceed. `alternatives` are disjunctive: satisfying ANY one suffices. The `requirements` within a single alternative are conjunctive: ALL must be satisfied. This OR-of-AND shape captures real authentication semantics such as 'OAuth2 OR (apiKey AND clientCert)', which a flat list cannot. A challenge is a SCOPE, not a hint: it bounds the context the invoker may receive. A runtime MUST provision only the context that satisfies the one selected alternative (the credentials it names plus non-secret configuration) and MUST NOT hand the invoker other stored credentials; an invoker MUST NOT receive raw context-store access (enumeration or arbitrary reads), only this scoped resolution.

target.description: The target the binding addresses — typically its endpoint URL or host. The runtime uses this to locate and store any context it resolves; how it derives a storage key from the target (for example, normalizing to host[:port] so families sharing a host share context) is the runtime's policy, not part of this contract. The invoker reports the target and neither keys nor stores.

alternatives.description: Disjunctive set of ways to satisfy the requirement. The runtime selects one alternative whose every requirement it can resolve, and provisions only that alternative's context, never the union of all alternatives and never unrelated stored credentials.

requirements.description: Conjunctive set: every requirement in this alternative must be resolved for the alternative to be satisfied.

InvocationError.description: Structured error carried by an `error` output frame. Indicates terminal failure of the invocation. Codes named by a rule of this contract (CONTEXT_REQUIRED, ERR_BINDING_NOT_FOUND, ERR_PROTOCOL, ERR_TRANSPORT_CLOSED, ERR_CANCELLED, ERR_VALIDATION_FAILED) are normative where named; other codes are recommended conventions (see the binding-invoker README's registry), and consumers SHOULD handle unknown codes gracefully.

code.description: Machine-readable error code (e.g., ERR_CANCELLED, ERR_VALIDATION_FAILED, ERR_PROTOCOL, CONTEXT_REQUIRED).

details.description: Optional structured details specific to the error code. For code CONTEXT_REQUIRED, `details` is a ContextRequiredDetails object.

OperationInvocationInput.description: Initial payload carried by invokeOperation's open frame: the interface to act on and which operation (or specific binding) to invoke, plus context.

operation.description: Operation name to invoke, resolved against the interface's flat identifier namespace — an operation key or any of its aliases, equally (OBI-T-12). A binding is selected per this interface's selection policy.

binding.description: Binding key to invoke; the operation is derived from it and selection is bypassed entirely. Mutually exclusive with operation.

OperationInvokerInputFrame.description: One frame from the caller into invokeOperation. Same shape as the binding-invoker input stream, with the open frame carrying an OperationInvocationInput: exactly one `open` first, then zero or more `input` frames, then exactly one `close`.

oneOf.title: open
oneOf.description: Open the invocation. MUST be the first frame on the input stream.

oneOf.title: input
oneOf.description: Write one input value to the operation. Validated against the operation's input schema.

oneOf.title: close
oneOf.description: Caller signals no more input frames will be sent. Idempotent.

OperationInvokerOutputFrame.description: One frame from invokeOperation back to the caller. Same shape as the binding-invoker output stream: zero or more `output`/`input_closed` frames, then exactly one terminal `complete` or `error`. Each output is validated against the operation's output schema.

oneOf.title: output
oneOf.description: One output value from the operation.

oneOf.title: input_closed
oneOf.description: The operation has closed the input side from its end. Subsequent caller input frames are ignored. Outputs may still flow.

oneOf.title: complete
oneOf.description: Terminal: the operation completed normally. MUST be the last frame.

oneOf.title: error
oneOf.description: Terminal: the operation failed. MUST be the last frame. Carries a structured InvocationError.

openbindings.operation-invoker.invokeOpe.description:
Invoke an operation on a supplied interface via a typed bidirectional I/O frame stream — the by-reference counterpart to binding-invoker's invokeBinding. The caller opens with an OperationInvocationInput that names a target `interface` plus either an `operation` key (the invoker selects a binding) or a specific `binding` key (the operation is derived from it). The invoker resolves the name to a concrete binding, validates values against the operation's declared input/output schemas, applies any binding transforms, and drives the underlying binding invocation. The frame protocol — one `open` carrying the OperationInvocationInput, then zero or more `input` frames, then `close`; zero or more `output`/`input_closed` frames then a single terminal `complete` or `error` — is identical to invokeBinding's, and every normative frame rule of binding-invoker.invokeBinding (first-frame-open, single-open, input-after-closure, exactly-one-terminal, transport-closure synthesis, discriminator dispatch, additionalProperties rejection) applies here unchanged. Cardinality is observed by how the caller drives the frames, not declared, so a request/response binding and a streaming binding satisfy the same operation identically.

## Operation-specific rules

1. **Binding selection.** Selection is this interface's configuration point. The candidate set is the operation's bindings whose governing binding specification the invoker can act on, natively or via a delegate. The default policy, which every implementation MUST implement: (a) non-deprecated candidates rank before deprecated ones; (b) within a tier, higher declared `preference` ranks first, and a candidate with no declared preference ranks below every candidate with one; (c) remaining ties break by lexicographic binding key. The policy is deterministic: the same document, the same supported set, and the same configuration select the same binding on every conforming implementation. Consumer configuration MAY override the default via `context.configuration.selection` — an ordered list of binding keys, the first invocable entry winning, with the default policy applying when no listed key is invocable. When the caller names a `binding`, that binding is used and selection is bypassed entirely. If no invocable binding exists, the invoker MUST emit a terminal `error` frame with code ERR_BINDING_NOT_FOUND.

2. **Schema validation.** Input values are validated against the resolved operation's input schema and outputs against its output schema, where declared. This is the layer binding-invoker lacks: invokeBinding treats values as opaque, invokeOperation knows the operation's schemas and transforms. The validation claim carries the core specification's claim semantics (OBI-T-16): success only against the complete statically reachable schema graph, the `format` keyword as annotation, applied per value; a mismatch is ERR_VALIDATION_FAILED, and an unresolvable governing schema graph is reported distinctly (ERR_SCHEMA_UNRESOLVED by convention), never as partial validation.

3. **Context is delegated, not owned.** invokeOperation does not resolve context itself. It forwards the caller-supplied `context` (a pass-through override) down to the selected binding invocation, which performs the target-keyed store resolution. A CONTEXT_REQUIRED error originating at the binding layer propagates up this operation's output stream unchanged, and the runtime resolves-and-retries against the operation. The target is a property of the resolved binding, so context resolution necessarily lives at the binding layer; see binding-invoker for the resolve-and-retry contract.

openbindings.operation-invoker.prepareOp.description: Operation-level preflight: report the context invoking an operation would require, without invoking it or causing side effects. The by-reference counterpart to binding-invoker's prepareBinding. The invoker resolves the named `operation` (or `binding`) on the supplied interface to a concrete binding — by the same selection rules as invokeOperation — and reports that binding's ContextRequiredDetails, or null when requirements cannot be determined without invoking. Returning null is always conformant, so no capability prevents a service from carrying the operation (correspondence remains per-operation, as for every contract operation). Like prepareBinding it is advisory — the reactive CONTEXT_REQUIRED error from invokeOperation is authoritative — and when `context` is supplied on the input, the result reports only the requirements still unsatisfied by it.
